headerLines: **Cadence:** Mon / Wed / Fri — all posts to FB + IG; X gets the same post trimmed or threaded
**Post types:** WHY (purpose/Golden Circle) · TIP (tactical) · POV (commentary/contrarian) · PROOF (credibility) · ASK (direct CTA)

**Audience:** Churches, Christian businesses, small-to-mid nonprofits, small business owners who'd rather build than market. Sensibility: mission-driven, stewardship-minded, skeptical of corporate marketing slop and Big Tech dependence. Values ownership, craftsmanship, permanence, and plain speech. Faith woven in naturally — never performative, never preachy.

**Framing spine:** Sinek's Golden Circle (Why → How → What) as the strategic frame, StoryBrand as the messaging frame. The two stack: your *why* is the story; your customer is the hero; clarity carries both. Mission-driven organizations have the strongest "why" in the room — most just bury it under a "what" website.

**Asset rule:** Every post must be fully producible by AI — text + generated graphics only (quote cards, tip cards, carousels, diagrams, stat graphics). No photos, video, screen recordings, or anything requiring a human behind a camera.
meta.standingNotes:
- **Voice:** Plain, direct, conviction without preachiness. Faith references natural and occasional — stewardship, calling, service, permanence — never forced into every post. Write like a craftsman, not a marketer. No hype vocabulary ("unlock," "supercharge," "game-changing").
- **Audience register:** Comfortable with ownership/anti-dependence framing (rented land, your list is yours, built to last), skeptical of corporate slop, respects competence and finished work. Never partisan, never culture-war bait — the sensibility shows in tone, not topics.
- **Golden Circle thread:** WHY posts bookend the quarter (Weeks 1 and 12); Why → How → What ordering appears in audits and frameworks throughout. Sinek and StoryBrand are cited by name where useful — familiar authorities for this audience.
- **Asset production (100% AI-generatable):** Five repeatable Poaster templates cover everything — quote card, tip/checklist card, carousel frame, diagram/wireframe, stat graphic. No photos, video, or screen captures anywhere in the plan.
- **X versions:** Hook + one supporting line; carousels become single strong lines or short threads. The ownership/craftsmanship posts (9/14, 8/19, 9/25) will travel furthest on X — prioritize those.
- **Hashtags:** 3–5 on IG (#churchmarketing #nonprofitmarketing #christianbusiness #smallbusinessmarketing base set), one or none on X, none on FB.
- **Engagement rule:** Reply to every comment within 24 hours.
- **Permission checks:** Weeks 10 and 12 PROOF posts use real client data/quotes — confirm with Tyler and clients before those slots lock.
- **Filler bank (swap-ins):** "Marketing terms in one sentence" series · platform-change reactions · one-post book summaries (Start With Why, Building a StoryBrand, Ogilvy) · "questions we get from churches" mini-series.

weeks.theme: Why an Agency in an AI Age
Integrating AI Without Losing Your Soul
Email Is the House You Own

posts.hook: People don't give to what you do. They give to why you do it.
posts.direction: Open the 90 days with the Golden Circle applied to mission-driven orgs. Sinek's insight lands harder for churches and nonprofits than anyone: your why *is* the offer. Most websites lead with programs and services (the what) and bury the mission. Graphic: Golden Circle diagram, Bequest brand colors.

posts.hook: Your organization has a stronger 'why' than any Fortune 500 brand. Your marketing should act like it.
posts.direction: Corporations invent purpose; ministries and mission-driven businesses have one. Yet their marketing is often the most timid in the room. Flip the frame: conviction is a competitive advantage.

posts.hook: The 5-second test: can a stranger tell why you exist, how you're different, and what to do next?
posts.direction: Homepage self-audit ordered Why → How → What. If a first-time visitor can't find the mission in 5 seconds, the site is a directory, not a message. Graphic: side-by-side wireframe, "what-first" vs. "why-first" homepage.

posts.hook: Your donor is the hero. Your church is the guide. Get those backwards and the story falls apart.
posts.direction: StoryBrand premise contextualized: the organization that positions itself as the hero of its own website loses the reader. Guides equip heroes. This is also just good theology of service — serve the person in front of you. Keep that note light.

posts.hook: Stop saying what you do. Start saying what changes because you exist.
posts.direction: Rewrite exercise, mission-flavored: "We provide community programs" → "Families in our city find help and hope." "Full-service marketing" → "Your mission reaches the people it was meant for." Carousel: 3 before/after rewrites (church, nonprofit, Christian business).

posts.hook: Every clear message answers 3 questions: Why do you exist? How does that help me? What do I do next?
posts.direction: Golden Circle and StoryBrand merged into one one-liner framework. Prompt readers to draft theirs in the comments. Engagement play. Graphic: 3-question template card.

posts.hook: You don't have a traffic problem. You have a stewardship problem.
posts.direction: Reframe conversion as stewardship — a category this audience already thinks in. Doubling your conversion rate doubles results from attention you already earned, without spending another dollar. Wasting existing visitors is the leak to fix first.

posts.hook: 7 conversion killers hiding on your website right now.
posts.direction: Carousel, one killer + fix per slide: slow load, buried CTA (or buried "Plan Your Visit"), insider jargon, no proof, mobile neglect, generic stock imagery, forms that interrogate. Examples alternate between business and church/nonprofit contexts.

posts.hook: Want a free 10-minute written teardown of your homepage?
posts.direction: First direct CTA. Offer a written homepage audit (delivered as a doc — AI-producible, no video) for the first several responders. Low-friction lead gen.

posts.hook: Honest answer from an agency that sells ads: some of you shouldn't buy them.
posts.direction: Trust anchor of the quarter. If your site doesn't convert, ads pour donor money into a leaky bucket — and mission-driven orgs answer for every dollar. Fix the funnel first. Honesty *is* the pitch.

posts.hook: The 3-question test before you spend a dime on ads.
posts.direction: (1) Are people already searching for what you offer? (2) Can the budget survive your niche's cost-per-click? (3) Does the landing page convert? Two noes = wait. Note the Google Ad Grant for eligible nonprofits — $10K/month most never claim properly.

posts.hook: Search ads catch demand. Social ads create it. Know which one your mission needs.
posts.direction: Education post: Google captures existing intent (people searching "church near me," "counseling in York"); Meta builds awareness for causes nobody knows to search for. Diagram graphic.

posts.hook: How much should a church, nonprofit, or small business actually spend on marketing? Here's the math.
posts.direction: Benchmarks by org type: businesses 5–10% of revenue to maintain, 10%+ to grow; nonprofits framed as cost-to-raise-a-dollar; churches framed as percentage of budget toward outreach. Concrete numbers, screenshot-able stat card.

posts.hook: The most expensive marketing is the cheap kind you have to redo.
posts.direction: The volunteer-built website, the DIY logo, the intern's ad account. Buy once, cry once — this audience already believes in paying for quality tools and finishing work properly. Costs run double: the redo plus every opportunity missed in between.

posts.hook: Where your first $1,000/month should go (in order).
posts.direction: Priority stack: 1) a website that converts, 2) your email list, 3) Google Business Profile / local visibility, 4) paid ads last. Counterintuitive ordering earns saves. Numbered-list graphic.

posts.weekTheme: Why an Agency in an AI Age
posts.hook: AI made content cheap. It made judgment expensive.
posts.direction: Anyone can generate 100 posts. Knowing which one serves your mission, your people, and your reputation — that didn't get automated. Wisdom over volume: a distinction this audience feels in its bones.

posts.weekTheme: Why an Agency in an AI Age
posts.hook: 'Can't ChatGPT just do our marketing?' Fair question. Here's our honest answer.
posts.direction: AI drafts; it doesn't know your congregation, own your results, or notice the ad account quietly burning money on a Saturday night. Tools need operators. We use AI heavily ourselves — openly — which is exactly why we know where it stops.

posts.weekTheme: Why an Agency in an AI Age
posts.hook: Big agency: 6 layers between you and the person doing the work. Bequest: you talk to the owners.
posts.direction: Comparison graphic (AI-generated diagram): big-agency org chart vs. two names. Small, direct, accountable — no account-manager telephone game. Craftsmanship over bureaucracy.

posts.weekTheme: Integrating AI Without Losing Your Soul
posts.hook: 5 places AI belongs in your organization's marketing — and 3 places it never should.
posts.direction: Belongs: research, first drafts, repurposing sermons/content into posts, data analysis, testing variants. Never: pastoral or donor correspondence, your final voice, anything published without human review. Carousel.

posts.weekTheme: Integrating AI Without Losing Your Soul
posts.hook: Automate the repeatable. Keep the relational.
posts.direction: The dividing line for churches and ministries: machines handle reporting, formatting, scheduling; humans keep the phone calls, the visits, the thank-you notes. Efficiency in service of presence, not instead of it.

posts.weekTheme: Integrating AI Without Losing Your Soul
posts.hook: We build AI-assisted marketing systems for organizations that don't have time to figure this out alone.
posts.direction: Direct pitch riding the week's theme. You've seen our take on where AI belongs — we'll build that line into your operation. CTA: book a call.

posts.hook: Your logo is not your brand. Your brand is what people say about you when you leave the room.
posts.direction: Logo = symbol; brand = reputation, promise, consistency. Orgs polish the mark and neglect the message. For a church, the "brand" is the community's testimony — protect that first.

posts.hook: 4 marks of a logo that will outlive the trend cycle.
posts.direction: Simple at small sizes, works in one color, distinct in your space, not chasing fads. This audience respects things built to last — cathedrals, not pop-ups. Graphic: timeless vs. trendy comparison (AI-generated abstract examples, no real trademarks).

posts.hook: Consistency beats brilliance. A B+ brand applied everywhere outperforms an A+ brand applied nowhere.
posts.direction: Audit prompt: do your website, email, bulletin, and social look like the same organization? Faithfulness in small things — the principle transfers. Checklist graphic.

posts.weekTheme: Email Is the House You Own
posts.hook: Social media is rented land. The algorithm is your landlord. Email is the house you own.
posts.direction: Ownership over dependence — this line was made for this audience. Platforms throttle reach and change rules overnight; nobody can take your list. ROI stat (~$36 per $1) as the kicker. Quote card.

posts.weekTheme: Email Is the House You Own
posts.hook: Your welcome email gets 4x the opens of anything else you'll ever send. Most orgs waste it.
posts.direction: Anatomy of a welcome sequence for a donor, visitor, or customer: deliver what you promised, tell them why you exist (the why again), give one next step. Template-outline graphic.

posts.weekTheme: Email Is the House You Own
posts.hook: Subject lines that get opened — without clickbait.
posts.direction: 5 formulas with mission-flavored examples: specific number ("3 families housed this month"), question, curiosity gap, how-to, plain benefit. Clickbait spikes opens once and spends trust you can't buy back.

posts.hook: Every campaign that works has 4 parts. Most that fail are missing #3.
posts.direction: Offer, audience, message, follow-up. #3 — message-audience fit — is the usual failure: the right cause pitched in the wrong words. Framework graphic, save-worthy.

posts.hook: Anatomy of a campaign: the numbers behind one that worked.
posts.direction: Anonymized stat-graphic walkthrough of real client results (permission-cleared): the goal, the sequence, the outcome, and one thing we'd change. All rendered as an AI-generated data graphic — no screenshots needed. Honesty about the weak spot builds more trust than a highlight reel.

posts.hook: One campaign finished beats five campaigns started.
posts.direction: Against spray-and-pray. Focus, frequency, follow-through — and the discipline to complete what you begin. Ties to why stretched-thin orgs need a partner who finishes.

posts.hook: The free channel most churches and local businesses ignore: your Google Business Profile.
posts.direction: GBP checklist: categories, service areas, weekly posts, review responses. "Church near me" and "plumber in [town]" are won or lost in the map pack, not on page one. Checklist graphic.

posts.hook: Reviews are the new word of mouth. Here's how to get more without begging.
posts.direction: Simple system: ask at the moment of gratitude, send the direct link, make it a 60-second task. One review a week compounds into local dominance. For churches: Google reviews are the new front door.

posts.hook: October is planning season. If 2027 is going to look different, the plan gets made now.
posts.direction: Seasonal urgency, no hype. Businesses set budgets; ministries plan year-end. CTA: strategy call before the calendar fills.

posts.hook: People don't buy from businesses or give to organizations. They trust people. Everything else is a tiebreaker.
posts.direction: Close the loop with the why: trust is built by clarity, consistency, proof, and follow-through — and it's the one asset that compounds. Return to the Golden Circle: people trust organizations whose why is legible.

posts.hook: Put testimonials next to the ask — not on a testimonials page nobody visits.
posts.direction: Social proof placement: beside donate buttons, on pricing, in signup flows. Proof answers the doubt at the exact moment it forms. Before/after wireframe graphic.

posts.hook: Their words, not ours.
posts.direction: Client testimonial rendered as a designed quote card (text only — fully AI-producible). Pull permission-cleared quotes. Minimal caption; let the quote work.

posts.hook: Nearly a third of annual giving happens in December. Your year-end campaign starts now — not Thanksgiving week.
posts.direction: Year-end giving timeline for ministries and nonprofits: October = plan and segment, November = warm the list with impact stories, December = the ask series. Timeline graphic.

posts.hook: The year-end email sequence that outperforms one big appeal.
posts.direction: The 5-email arc: story → impact → ask → deadline → last call. Each email one job. Outline graphic — actionable enough to steal, which is the point.

posts.hook: We have room for 2 more clients before year-end. If your mission deserves marketing that finally works, let's talk.
posts.direction: True scarcity (two-man shop, real capacity). Close the 90 days where they began: your why is your strongest asset — we make sure people can see it. CTA: contact link.
